title: Use of There (There is / There are / There was...)
description: Learn how to use 'There' to indicate existence — There is, There are, There was, There were, There will be, There has been. One of the most frequently used structures in English for describing surroundings, situations, and facts.

subtopics.title: There Is — Singular
subtopics.description: There is a book on the table. Describing existence of one thing.

subtopics.title: There Are — Plural
subtopics.description: There are 20 students. There are many options. Multiple items.

subtopics.title: There Was / Were — Past
subtopics.description: There was a problem. There were many people. Past existence.

subtopics.title: There Will Be — Future
subtopics.description: There will be a meeting tomorrow. Future situations and events.

subtopics.title: There Has Been / Have Been
subtopics.description: There has been a change. There have been many complaints. Perfect.

subtopics.title: There in Questions
subtopics.description: Is there a problem? Are there any seats? Was there an issue?

subtopics.title: There in Negatives
subtopics.description: There is no milk. There aren't any seats. There was no time.

subtopics.title: There in Daily Life
subtopics.description: 100+ real sentences describing rooms, cities, situations, surroundings.

subtopics.title: There in Professional English
subtopics.description: There is an opportunity. There has been a delay. Office communication.

subtopics.title: There vs Their vs They're
subtopics.description: The three confusing homophones — differences, rules, and examples.

subtopics.title: Common Errors
subtopics.description: There is many people (wrong!), Their is a book (wrong!) — corrections.

learningObjectives: Use There is for singular nouns to describe existence
Use There are for plural nouns
Use There was/were for past situations
Use There will be for future situations
Use There has been / have been for recent changes
Form questions with Is there / Are there / Was there
Form negatives with There is no / There aren't any
Describe rooms, places, and situations using there structures
Correctly spell and use there vs their vs they're
Use There structures naturally in professional communication
